Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time on weekends, but with everything going on, I'd end up cramming whenever I could find a free hour. Being a kinesthetic learner, I needed to actually do things to understand them, so I'd spend a lot of time working through problems physically rather than just reading. Sometimes I'd plan to study but then feel too overwhelmed to start, so the routine never really stuck.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how stressed I was feeling. When deadlines were close, I'd suddenly be studying way more intensely, but during quieter weeks I'd slack off. I know that's not ideal, but with my attendance being inconsistent and feeling scared about falling behind, it was hard to maintain anything regular. The anxiety would sometimes paralyze me into doing nothing, then I'd panic and cram.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification or that creeping fear that I was missing something important. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were new announcements because I was worried about being caught off guard. The fear of failing or missing crucial information drove most of my logins, honestly. It wasn't really excitement about learning—more like damage control.
Q4: There were a few weeks where I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee. It happened when I was feeling more in control of things, usually after the CBT sessions helped me manage my anxiety a bit. But that routine would break down whenever stress piled up from other responsibilities or when I felt too scared to face what might be waiting for me on the platform.
Q5: The video content was genuinely helpful for me—I watched almost 300 minutes worth because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb things at my own pace. Discussion posts were also useful because interacting with peers made concepts stick better for me. Since I learn best by doing and engaging, those interactive elements felt more natural than just reading through static materials.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials and some of the supplementary resources. Sitting still and reading just doesn't work for my brain—I zone out and retain nothing. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. Some resources also felt overwhelming when I was already stressed, so I'd avoid them entirely rather than face that anxious feeling.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see direct results or when it connected to something I'd actually be tested on. If I could physically work through a problem or discuss it with someone, that felt productive. But if it was just passive consumption with no clear payoff, my motivation—which is already pretty low—would tank completely.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford when I was barely keeping up with required ones. There was always this voice in my head saying I should do them, but then the fear of not finishing mandatory work would win out. Required activities had that urgency that forced me to act, while optional ones just added to my guilt pile without the deadline pressure to push through.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download videos and PDFs to study offline because sometimes I needed to work through things without the distraction of being online. I also used my own notes heavily—writing things out by hand helps me remember better than typing. I'd practice problems on paper, work through concepts physically, and none of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were whole study sessions where I was working from printed materials or downloaded content, going through practice problems manually. I'd spend hours with my notes spread out on my desk, actively learning, but the LMS would show nothing. Sometimes I'd watch a video once online, then replay it from a downloaded copy multiple times—only that first view counted.
Q11: Yeah, this was pretty common for me. I'd access a lecture video or reading once to download it, then study from it offline for days. It was actually better for my anxiety to work offline without seeing all the other tasks I hadn't completed staring at me. The LMS would show one brief login, but I'd have spent hours with that material afterward.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. I was scared constantly—scared of failing, scared of falling further behind, scared of disappointing everyone. My attendance dropped, and every time I thought about the course, this wave of dread would hit me. The CBT sessions helped some, but there were weeks where just opening my laptop felt impossible because I knew I'd see how behind I was.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed about other things, the LMS became this thing I'd avoid because it represented all my failures. Competing responsibilities from extracurriculars and life stuff meant I'd sometimes go days without logging in. The fatigue was real—medium stress sounds manageable, but combined with low motivation and feeling scared, it was paralyzing.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd scroll through materials with my eyes glazed over, technically 'accessing' them but not absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd have a video playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about everything I hadn't done. The engagement numbers might look okay, but my brain wasn't actually there.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The fear of doing them badly made me avoid starting at all—classic anxiety response, I know. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more capable, but tomorrow I'd feel the same scared paralysis. My assignment completion rate shows exactly how bad this got.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced action. I'd ignore the LMS for days, then suddenly be on there frantically when something was due soon. It wasn't healthy—lots of last-minute panic sessions. Without that external pressure, my low motivation and fear would keep me away. Deadlines were terrifying but necessary.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. Good feedback would give me a brief boost, but negative feedback would send me spiraling into more fear and avoidance. Sometimes I'd delay looking at feedback because I was scared of what it might say. When I did get constructive comments, it helped me focus my limited study time better, but emotionally it was always a risk.
Q18: The course structure with 20 online modules was overwhelming for someone with my motivation levels. Having so much content made it hard to know where to focus, and I'd freeze up trying to decide what to prioritize. The flexibility was theoretically good, but without strong internal motivation, I needed more guidance than the structure provided.
Q19: The video content being available for download made engagement easier—I could work in my own way offline. But the sheer volume of materials and the way assignments piled up made things harder. I wish there had been more hands-on, practical activities built into the LMS itself, since that's how I learn best. The discussion boards were good but sometimes felt disconnected from the main content.
Q20: I think that's often fear and anxiety at work. You're logging in, trying to engage, maybe watching videos or reading discussions, but the actual assignments feel too scary to attempt. You're circling the work without landing on it. I've definitely been there—high activity that's really just anxious checking and avoidance of the hard stuff.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My exam score was decent even though my LMS activity and assignment completion were inconsistent. I did a lot of learning offline that never showed up in the logs. Some students just process information differently or study in ways the system can't track. Performance and logged activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline work. They can't capture whether you're actually understanding or just scrolling anxiously. They definitely can't show the emotional state behind the behavior—whether someone's engaged or just scared and checking obsessively.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just going through motions. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, applying concepts—usually when I'm doing something hands-on. Real engagement feels different from anxiety-driven activity. It's hard to achieve when you're scared and unmotivated, but when it happens, learning actually sticks.
Q24: The LMS can't show how scared I've been throughout this course, or how much that fear has shaped every interaction I've had with it. It can't show the offline studying, the physical note-taking, the times I was learning but too anxious to log in. It also misses how the CBT sessions affected my engagement patterns, or how my low resilience made every setback feel catastrophic. The numbers tell a story, but not my story.
